name: Christopher Booker's 7 Basic Plots
core_claim: Booker's central claim (in The Seven Basic Plots: Why We Tell Stories, 2004) is that beneath the surface variety of all narrative there lie a small, finite number of recurring deep structures — seven master plots — which recur across cultures and millennia because they encode universal psychological patterns. Drawing explicitly on Jungian archetype theory, Booker argues that what drives narrative engagement is the resonance of a story with these archetypal patterns of the unconscious: the seven plots dramatise a single underlying movement from a state of constriction, division, or domination by a 'dark power' (egotism, the immature self) toward integration, wholeness, and the triumph of the 'light' (the mature, balanced Self). The plots are: Overcoming the Monster, Rags to Riches, The Quest, Voyage and Return, Comedy, Tragedy, and Rebirth. Each (except in their tragic/'dark' inversions) traces a hero or heroine's psychological maturation; Tragedy is the archetype's failure mode, where the protagonist is consumed by the dark power rather than transcending it. The deepest claim is therefore not taxonomic but moral-psychological: stories enthral us because they rehearse the integration of the Self, and a story 'works' to the degree it completes this archetypal pattern.
descriptive_vs_experiential: Primarily STRUCTURAL, with a strong MOTIVATIONAL/values-based substrate, and only weakly descriptive-topical. It is NOT experiential/affective in the sense of capturing how a story feels moment-to-moment (no tracking of suspense, fear, joy, poignancy as felt states). The seven plots are deep-structure templates — sequences of narrative function (a threat appears, a hero is called, an ordeal is faced, integration is achieved) — so the framework operates at the level of plot architecture / story shape, the same altitude as Propp's morphology or the monomyth. Layered beneath this is a values/motivational claim (the move from ego-domination to Self-integration), which is closer to a moral-developmental dimension than an affective one. It says relatively little about a story's TOPIC (a Quest can be about treasure, knowledge, or a person; an Overcoming-the-Monster can be a dragon, a shark, a dictator, or a disease), so it is a poor descriptive/topical taxonomy. Best read as: structural archetype primary, embedded moral-psychological telos secondary.
key_dimensions: The primary axis is a categorical typology of SEVEN plot archetypes: (1) Overcoming the Monster — protagonist confronts and defeats a threatening evil/antagonistic force; (2) Rags to Riches — a humble protagonist rises, (typically) loses everything, then regains it transformed and matured; (3) The Quest — a hero and companions journey toward a distant, vital goal against obstacles; (4) Voyage and Return — protagonist enters a strange/abnormal world, is tested, escapes and returns changed; (5) Comedy — a tangle of confusion, misunderstanding, and division is progressively resolved into recognition, reconciliation, and (often) union; (6) Tragedy — a protagonist pursues a dark/forbidden desire through stages of obsession to destruction; (7) Rebirth — a protagonist falls under a dark spell/state and is redeemed/awakened by a redemptive force. Cross-cutting these are several secondary dimensions Booker proposes: (a) a shared FIVE-STAGE meta-structure (Anticipation -> Dream -> Frustration -> Nightmare -> Resolution); (b) a LIGHT vs DARK inversion of each plot (the 'dark' or unredeemed version where the pattern fails — Tragedy is effectively the dark inversion of the maturation arc); (c) the 'Rule of Three' (cumulative/ascending/contrasting/dialectical repetition, with transformation triggered on the third occurrence); (d) the underlying archetypal opposition of ego/dark-power vs Self/light/wholeness; (e) recurring archetypal roles (Hero, Dark Father/Mother, Light/Dark figures, the anima/animus 'other half'). The candidate vocabulary additions are the seven plot slugs plus the light/dark valence flag.
cross_cultural_validity: Booker ASSERTS universality strongly — he claims the seven plots recur across all human cultures and historical periods, and the Jungian collective-unconscious framing is explicitly a claim of cross-cultural, trans-historical invariance. [uncertain] However, this universality is asserted, not empirically demonstrated cross-culturally. The book's evidence base is overwhelmingly the Western canon (Greek myth, the Bible, European fairy tales, Shakespeare, Hollywood film) with selective gestures toward other traditions; it is implicitly Western and Jungian in its assumptions. Critics note the framework's individualistic, hero-centred, teleological 'maturation of the Self' arc carries Western (and specifically Jungian/Romantic) moral assumptions about selfhood and resolution that may not map onto narrative traditions privileging cyclical time, collective rather than individual protagonists, non-resolving or open structures, or different moral teloi (e.g. some East-Asian, Indigenous, and oral-tradition forms). What plausibly DOES travel: the broad structural shapes (a threat overcome, a journey out and back) overlap with independently-derived universals (the monomyth; Propp's functions on Russian folktales), suggesting some structures are widespread. What breaks down: the prescriptive moral telos (that a 'true' story must end in integration/wholeness), and the forced fitting of works that resist resolution — the same rigidity critics flagged within the Western canon would worsen cross-culturally. No formal cross-linguistic psychometric validation exists; this is literary-critical, not validated social science.
empirical_grounding: Weak by social-science standards. The framework is a work of literary criticism / interpretive theory built over ~34 years of close reading, not an empirically tested model. Its support is the author's demonstration that a large corpus of canonical works can be mapped onto the seven types — which is illustrative, not confirmatory, and vulnerable to confirmation bias and post-hoc fitting (the central methodological criticism: any sufficiently flexible typology can 'explain' most stories). There is no experimental, correlational, or pre-registered evidence linking Booker's plot categories to enjoyment, completion, or preference data. The most relevant ADJACENT empirical work is COMPUTATIONAL and concerns emotional arcs rather than Booker's plots directly: Reagan et al. (2016), 'The emotional arcs of stories are dominated by six basic shapes' (1,327 Project Gutenberg texts, sentiment time-series, SVD + clustering + supervised/unsupervised methods) found six dominant emotional-trajectory shapes (Rise; Fall; Fall-Rise 'man in a hole'; Rise-Fall 'Icarus'; Rise-Fall-Rise 'Cinderella'; Fall-Rise-Fall 'Oedipus'). This validates that a SMALL NUMBER of underlying shapes exists, but it measures AFFECTIVE VALENCE TRAJECTORY, not Booker's structural/archetypal plot types — and the authors explicitly note plots and emotional arcs are distinct (two different Booker plots can share one arc; one plot can take several arcs). So Reagan et al. corroborate the 'few underlying shapes' intuition while NOT validating Booker's specific seven. To validate LLM-extracted Booker labels you would need: (a) human gold-standard plot-type annotations of a work set (with inter-annotator agreement — itself likely low given the categories' fuzziness/overlap), (b) LLM plot-type predictions from synopses, (c) agreement metrics against the humans, and ideally (d) correlation of plot type against real preference/completion data to test whether the category carries any predictive signal for enjoyment.
operationalisability: MODERATELY extractable from a synopsis — better than affective-response frameworks, worse than topical tags — but with significant reliability problems. A plot summary or logline usually carries enough structural signal for an LLM to assign a plausible primary plot type: 'a young farm boy must destroy an evil empire' reads as Overcoming the Monster; 'enters a strange world, is tested, returns home' reads as Voyage and Return. The natural extraction unit is the WHOLE WORK (these are gestalt deep structures), though long-form serialised TV may carry one plot type per season/arc, and many works are MULTI-PLOT (a film can be simultaneously a Quest and an Overcoming-the-Monster — Booker himself notes plots combine). Failure modes: (1) Category overlap and fuzziness — Rags to Riches, The Quest, and Overcoming the Monster share structure; reliable single-label assignment is hard and inter-rater agreement would be low, so the LLM will produce plausible-but-arbitrary picks on ambiguous cases. (2) Multi-plot and genre-hybrid works force a false single choice unless multi-label is allowed. (3) The LIGHT/DARK valence and the 'integration vs destruction' telos often live in the ENDING, which a spoiler-free synopsis hides — so Tragedy vs a triumphant arc, or a redemptive vs unredeemed Rebirth, may be invisible. (4) Confirmation-bias amplification — an LLM, like Booker, can rationalise almost any synopsis into one of seven boxes, producing confident but unfalsifiable labels. (5) Comedy is especially weakly signalled by plot synopsis (it is as much a tonal/structural mode as a plot). (6) The deep archetypal/moral reading (ego vs Self) is essentially NOT extractable — only the surface structural shape is. Recommend: treat as a multi-label, low-to-moderate-confidence structural tag, never single-label, and never as ground truth for the maturation telos.
motivation_served: Booker's own answer is eudaimonic-developmental: stories serve a deep psychological need to rehearse the integration and maturation of the Self — meaning, moral orientation, and a sense of wholeness — rather than mere hedonic pleasure (indeed he is dismissive of works that deliver pleasure without the 'true' archetypal resolution). So the framework maps most naturally onto eudaimonic appreciation and identity/meaning needs, and to mood-regulation insofar as a completed archetypal arc is satisfying/cathartic. For GROUP viewing it is of LIMITED direct use as a motivation-matcher (it does not model viewer-side wants), but plot-type preference can act as a shared-taste signal: two people who both gravitate to Quest/Voyage-and-Return adventures, or who both dislike Tragedy, have a tractable overlap. Its more useful group role is as a structural compatibility/variety axis ('we keep watching grim Tragedies; let's pick an Overcoming-the-Monster crowd-pleaser') rather than as a deep motivational model.
receiver_dependence: The seven plot types are predominantly PROPERTIES OF THE WORK ALONE — the structural shape of a story (a journey out and back; a monster defeated) is largely receiver-independent and reasonably stable across viewers, much like a genre signal. This makes the plot-type label an ABSOLUTE-ish axis suitable for content tagging. However, two layers are RELATIONAL: (1) the archetypal RESONANCE / felt meaningfulness Booker claims as the driver of engagement is an interaction between the work and a specific viewer's psyche, mood, and values — whether a Rebirth arc 'moves you' depends on you; (2) plot-type PREFERENCE (does this viewer like Tragedies? do they crave Quests?) is a viewer-side trait, not a property of any work, and must come from ratings/onboarding, not from a synopsis. So encode separately: work-level plot-type tags (synopsis-extractable, roughly absolute) versus per-user plot-type affinities (behavioural data, per-viewer). For a two-person recommender the work-level tags are the shareable, comparable layer; the resonance/telos claims are not directly usable as work properties.
blind_spots: (1) It captures structure, not topic — tells you a story is a Quest but nothing about WHAT the quest concerns (love, revenge, treasure, knowledge), so it cannot stand alone for content matching. (2) It is silent on affective texture and tone — two Overcoming-the-Monster stories can be a children's cartoon or a bleak horror; the felt experience (fear, fun, dread, warmth) is invisible. (3) The rigid prescriptive telos (a 'real' story must reach integration/wholeness) systematically mis-handles ambiguous, ironic, open-ended, modernist, anti-narrative, and slice-of-life works — the documented criticism (Mars-Jones) that Booker dismisses Chekhov, Proust, Joyce, Kafka, Lawrence while praising conventional Hollywood fare. (4) Confirmation bias / unfalsifiability — seven flexible boxes can absorb almost anything, so the typology risks explaining everything and predicting nothing. (5) Multi-plot and hybrid works are forced into single categories. (6) Category overlap (Rags-to-Riches / Quest / Overcoming-the-Monster) undermines clean discrimination. (7) Ensemble, mosaic, plotless, procedural-episodic, and experimental structures fit poorly. (8) It says nothing about spectacle, aesthetics, humour craft, parasocial attachment, rewatchability, or nostalgia. (9) The Jungian metaphysics is untestable and not all readers accept the moral framing. (10) Comedy as defined is closer to a structural mode than a peer 'plot', sitting awkwardly with the others.
vocabulary_implications: Add a small, controlled STRUCTURAL facet 'plot-archetype' that is orthogonal to the existing topical theme slugs and MULTI-LABEL (a work can carry more than one). Proposed slugs: overcoming-the-monster, rags-to-riches, the-quest, voyage-and-return, comedy-resolution, tragedy, rebirth. Pair each work with: (a) one-to-three plot-archetype labels (multi-label, low/moderate confidence, LLM-extracted from synopsis); (b) an optional light-vs-dark / integration-vs-destruction valence flag — but FLAG this as frequently NOT synopsis-extractable because it depends on the ending, so default to null rather than guessing. Explicitly NOT extractable / out of scope: the Jungian 'ego vs Self maturation' deep reading and the prescriptive 'true story' telos — do not encode these as work properties. Add a PER-USER plot-archetype-affinity vector (e.g. tolerance for tragedy, appetite for quests) populated from ratings/onboarding — a different data source, not from synopsis. Guardrails for extraction: require multi-label to avoid false single picks; surface a confidence score; treat output as a structural prior, ideally fused with genre and emotional-arc signals (cf. Reagan et al. six shapes), never as ground truth. Note these slugs are STRUCTURE, not topic, and must not replace or be merged into the topical theme list.
relationship_to_existing_vocabulary: Booker's plots are STRUCTURAL deep-shapes and sit at a different altitude from most existing slugs, which are topical/thematic — but several existing slugs are effectively informal surface-instances of his archetypes, revealing both overlap and a missing structural layer. Direct correspondences: 'underdog-rise' and 'rise-and-fall' are near-synonyms for Rags-to-Riches (and its dark inversion); 'rise-and-fall' also overlaps Tragedy. 'survival-and-endurance', 'chase-and-escape' and much of 'mystery-investigation'/'whodunit'/'heist-and-caper' frequently instantiate The Quest or Overcoming the Monster. 'fish-out-of-water' and post-apocalyptic/dystopian arrival stories often realise Voyage and Return. 'redemption-arc', 'guilt-and-atonement', and 'addiction-and-recovery' are strong Rebirth markers. 'coming-of-age' cuts across Rags-to-Riches/Quest/Voyage-and-Return (the maturation telos Booker centres). 'vengeance-and-its-cost', 'power-corrupts', and 'moral-compromise' frequently realise Tragedy. The framework VALIDATES that the vocabulary already gestures at these shapes piecemeal but lacks an explicit, controlled STRUCTURAL facet — the gap is a clean plot-archetype layer rather than today's scattered structural-ish slugs ('underdog-rise', 'rise-and-fall', 'chase-and-escape') living in the same flat list as topical and texture tags. It suggests a MISALIGNMENT: structure, topic, texture, and affective gratification are currently conflated in one list and should be separated into orthogonal facets. It does NOT invalidate the topical slugs; it argues for adding a structural facet alongside them. Comedy-as-plot also overlaps the 'absurdist-comedy'/'dark-comedy' texture tags but is structural where those are tonal — another reason to keep facets distinct.
key_sources: Booker, Christopher (2004). The Seven Basic Plots: Why We Tell Stories. Continuum (London/New York). ISBN 0826452094. Primary source; book, not freely available (paywalled/purchase). Overview and criticism: Wikipedia, 'The Seven Basic Plots' (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Seven_Basic_Plots, free) — includes Adam Mars-Jones's criticism re rigidity. Adjacent computational validation of 'few underlying shapes': Reagan, Mitchell, Kiley, Danforth & Dodds (2016), 'The emotional arcs of stories are dominated by six basic shapes,' EPJ Data Science 5:31 — arXiv free PDF https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.07772 and https://arxiv.org/pdf/1606.07772; open-access EPJ version. Related broader work: Dodds et al., 'Towards a science of human stories: using sentiment analysis and emotional arcs...' (https://arxiv.org/pdf/1712.06163, free). Accessible secondary criticism: Rhiannon Grant, 'Why Tell Stories? Christopher Booker's Seven Basic Plots and some of his mistakes' (https://brigidfoxandbuddha.wordpress.com/2023/05/30/, free, blog). MIT Technology Review summary of Reagan et al. (https://www.technologyreview.com/2016/07/06/158961/, free).
